mnemonics.title: INHERIT
mnemonics.content: I - Is-A relationship, N - New class (Derived), H - Hierarchical inheritance, E - Extends base class, R - Reusability, I - Inherited members, T - Types: Single, Multiple, Multilevel

mnemonics.title: P.O.L.Y.M.O.R.P.H.I.S.M
mnemonics.content: P - Polymorphism means 'Many Forms', O - Overloading (compile-time), L - Late binding (runtime), Y - Virtual functions use vtable, M - Method overriding, O - Operator overloading, R - Run-time vs Compile-time, P - Pure virtual = abstract, H - Hybrid inheritance complications, I - Interface through abstract class, S - Static binding default, M - Memory address via pointer

mnemonics.title: A.P.E
mnemonics.content: A - Access specifiers: Private (hidden), P - Protected (accessible to derived), E - Public (accessible everywhere)

mnemonics.title: VIRTUAL
mnemonics.content: V - Virtual keyword enables override, I - Indirect function call via pointer, R - Runtime polymorphism achieved, T - Table (vtable) stores function addresses, U - Using virtual = dynamic binding, A - Abstract class has pure virtual, L - Late binding occurs at runtime,

acronyms.usage: Inheritance and Polymorphism are pillars of OOP along with Encapsulation and Abstraction
A lookup table used at runtime to resolve function calls for virtual functions. Each class with virtual functions has its own vtable
Used with polymorphism to identify the actual type of object at runtime using dynamic_cast and typeid
Describes inheritance: A Derived class IS-A type of Base class (e.g., Dog IS-A Animal)
Describes composition/aggregation: A class HAS-A member of another class
Virtual function with =0; Makes class Abstract. Must be overridden by derived classes

visualTips.visualization: Picture a family tree: Grandparent (base) → Parent (derived from grandparent) → Child (derived from parent). Properties and methods flow downward like inherited traits
Think of a remote control with same button name but different actions based on how long you press it - same name, different parameters (compile-time decision)
Imagine a magic mirror that shows different reflections based on who stands in front of it. The same function call shows different behavior based on actual object type
Think of a blueprint or template - you can't build a house from just blueprints, but you can build houses following that blueprint. Abstract classes define what derived classes MUST do
A TV remote (base pointer) that can control any TV (derived objects). Same remote, different TV behaviors when you press buttons

analogies.analogy: A child inherits eye color from one parent only
A child inherits surname from father and cultural traditions from mother - two parents contribute
Grandfather's house → Father's house → Your house. Each level inherits from the previous
A generic 'open' instruction at a school: principal opens assembly, teacher opens class, gatekeeper opens gate - same word 'open' but different actions based on who performs it
A restaurant menu (base class) lists 'Today's Special' but the actual dish depends on what the kitchen prepares that day (derived class implementation)
A job posting (abstract class) says 'Must have degree' - it doesn't specify which degree, but any candidate (derived class) must have SOME degree to qualify
Converting a specific dog's ID to a general animal ID - you're seeing it as a more general category
From general 'Animal' category, specifically identifying it as a 'Dog' - narrowing down the type
